info.set_name: Gastrointestinal Comprehensive Set 2: Digestion and Absorption (MCQ)

q1.prompt: A newborn is diagnosed with a rare genetic mutation that renders the SGLT1 transporter non-functional. Which of the following dietary modifications would be most effective in allowing the infant to absorb carbohydrate energy while preventing severe osmotic diarrhoea?

options.label: Use of a formula containing exclusively corn starch.
Supplementation of every feed with oral lactase tablets.
Replacement of dietary glucose with sucrose.
Use of a formula where fructose is the only carbohydrate source.
Intravenous infusion of insulin with every feeding to promote uptake.

q2.prompt: A patient is found to have a total congenital deficiency of enterokinase (enteropeptidase) in the duodenal brush border. Which of the following outcomes is the most likely physiological consequence?

options.label: Inability to activate any of the major pancreatic proteases.
Impairment of the activation of pepsinogen to pepsin in the stomach.
A compensatory increase in the endocytosis of intact proteins via M cells.
An increased rate of starch digestion by pancreatic amylase to compensate.
Normal protein digestion due to the independent action of chymotrypsin.

q3.prompt: A patient with a gastrin-secreting tumor (Zollinger-Ellison syndrome) presents with severe steatorrhoea. What is the primary physiological explanation for this fat malabsorption?

options.label: Excessive gastrin directly inhibits the movement of micelles to the brush border.
High levels of gastrin stimulate a massive increase in the bile acid pool, causing soap formation.
Gastric H^+ secretion is so low that it fails to activate pro-colipase in the lumen.
The trophic effect of gastrin on the colon interferes with the exit of chylomicrons.
The low duodenal pH denatures pancreatic lipase and causes bile salts to precipitate.

q4.prompt: A patient is diagnosed with abetalipoproteinemia. After a meal containing triglycerides, a biopsy of the intestinal mucosa is performed. What is the predicted histological finding within the enterocytes?

options.label: Complete absence of smooth endoplasmic reticulum.
Total depletion of intracellular fatty acid-binding proteins (FABPs).
Hypertrophy of the Golgi apparatus filled with secretory vesicles.
The cells are engorged with re-esterified triglycerides and lipids.
Atrophy of the brush border with a significant loss of microvillar height.

q5.prompt: In a research experiment, the Na^+ in the intestinal lumen is completely replaced by an equal concentration of K^+. Which of the following is the predicted effect on the transport of fructose and glucose?

options.label: Fructose transport will stop, but glucose transport will be unaffected.
Glucose transport will decrease significantly, while fructose transport will be unaffected.
Both fructose and glucose transport will be completely abolished.
Glucose transport will increase in rate to compensate for the loss of Na^+.
Both sugars will now be absorbed via K^+-dependent secondary active transport.

q6.prompt: During a physiological study, it is noted that the absorption of a specific dipeptide is significantly more rapid than the absorption of its constituent free amino acids. This observation is best explained by:

options.label: The utilization of an H^+ gradient by the PepT1 transporter for peptide uptake.
The higher density of Na^+-amino acid cotransporters compared to peptide carriers.
The fact that dipeptides skip the cytosolic peptidase step.
The presence of specific basolateral transporters for all tripeptides.
The inhibition of amino acid carriers by the presence of luminal H^+.

q7.prompt: A 65-year-old patient with severe Crohn's disease undergoes a surgical resection of the terminal 100 cm of the ileum. Which of the following is the most likely long-term consequence regarding the liver?

options.label: A decrease in the fecal excretion of bile salts.
An increase in the concentration of bile salts in the portal blood.
Permanent relaxation of the gallbladder due to a loss of CCK.
An increase in the synthesis of primary bile acids from cholesterol.
A reduction in the total bile acid pool that becomes unresponsive to hepatic feedback.

q8.prompt: A patient with chronic pancreatitis has severely decreased pancreatic lipase secretion. Which of the following will be the most significantly affected component of their nutritional status?

options.label: Absorption of water-soluble B-complex vitamins via Na^+-cotransport.
Uptake of medium-chain fatty acids into the portal blood.
Digestion of internal peptide bonds in dietary proteins.
Initial hydrolysis of starch by salivary amylase in the mouth.
Solubilization and absorption of fat-soluble vitamins (A, D, E, K).

q9.prompt: Hartnup's disease is characterized by a deficiency in the Na^+-dependent transporter for neutral amino acids. Despite this, patients often show few signs of protein malnutrition. What is the physiological reason for this?

options.label: Neutral amino acids are non-essential and can be synthesized from glucose.
Neutral amino acids are still absorbed efficiently as dipeptides and tripeptides.
The stomach enzyme pepsin can completely compensate for the brush-border defect.
Intestinal bacteria synthesize neutral amino acids and release them into the colon for absorption.
Neutral amino acids are primarily absorbed by facilitated diffusion via GLUT2.

q10.prompt: In an oral glucose tolerance test, 50g of glucose is administered. A subject with a congenital GLUT2 deficiency is compared to a healthy control. What is the expected blood glucose profile in the GLUT2-deficient subject?

options.label: A flattened blood glucose curve because glucose is trapped inside the enterocytes.
A much more rapid spike in blood glucose due to faster exit from the basolateral membrane.
A normal curve because SGLT1 can also transport glucose directly into the blood.
A massive increase in blood glucose because SGLT1 is up-regulated in the absence of GLUT2.
A curve identical to a patient with Type II diabetes and insulin resistance.

q11.prompt: Colipase is a critical cofactor for lipid digestion secreted by the pancreas. What is its specific function in the duodenal lumen?

options.label: It directly hydrolyzes the 2-position ester bond of phospholipids.
It acts as a detergent to emulsify large fat globules into 1-micron droplets.
It binds to Apoprotein B to facilitate the packaging of chylomicrons.
It converts primary bile acids into more soluble secondary bile salts.
It prevents the inactivation of pancreatic lipase by displacing inhibitory bile salts.

q12.prompt: Which of the following describes the correct temporal order of protein digestion and absorption?

options.label: Activation of trypsinogen -> action of pepsin -> brush border hydrolysis -> peptide transport.
Peptide transport -> activation of trypsinogen -> action of pepsin -> cytosolic peptidase action.
Pancreatic protease action -> peptide transport -> action of pepsin -> brush border hydrolysis.
Action of pepsin -> peptide transport -> pancreatic protease action -> brush border hydrolysis.
Action of pepsin -> activation of trypsinogen -> pancreatic protease action -> peptide transport.

q13.prompt: A patient experiences severe osmotic diarrhoea and flatulence shortly after eating a bowl of cereal with milk. An H2-breath test is positive. This condition is primarily a result of:

options.label: Failure of the SGLT1 transporter to move glucose across the apical membrane.
The inability of colonic bacteria to ferment unabsorbed disaccharides.
Rapid gastric emptying of hypertonic chyme into the terminal ileum.
Undigested lactose in the lumen increasing the osmotic pressure and holding water.
Inhibition of the Na^+/K^+ ATPase by the presence of luminal sugars.

q14.prompt: Medium-chain fatty acids (C6-C12) are often used in clinical nutrition for patients with malabsorption. How does their absorption differ from long-chain fatty acids?

options.label: They are water-soluble and can enter the portal blood directly as free fatty acids.
They require micelle formation but not chylomicron packaging for exit.
They are re-esterified into triglycerides in the smooth ER before exiting the cell.
They must enter the lacteals bound to apoprotein B within chylomicrons.
Their absorption is strictly dependent on the presence of the master switch trypsin.

q15.prompt: Trypsin is often called the "master switch" of pancreatic protein digestion. This is because trypsin:

options.label: Is the only protease capable of digesting meat collagen.
Is the only protease that functions at an acidic pH in the duodenal bulb.
Directly activates itself and all other pancreatic pro-proteases.
Is secreted in its active form by the gastric chief cells to ensure early digestion.
Inhibits the action of enterokinase to prevent over-digestion of the mucosa.

q16.prompt: During the digestion of a meal containing phospholipids, which pancreatic enzyme is responsible for yielding lysophosphatidylcholine and a free fatty acid?

options.label: Pancreatic lipase.
Phospholipase A2.
Cholesterol ester hydrolase.
Alpha-dextrinase.
Lingual lipase.

q17.prompt: In the "Physiolego" of carbohydrate absorption, the SGLT1 transporter is considered a secondary active transporter. What is the immediate consequence of administering an inhibitor of the Na^+/K^+ ATPase (like ouabain) to the enterocyte?

options.label: Fructose absorption will increase via SGLT1 to compensate for the loss of glucose.
Glucose will be transported into the cell in exchange for K^+ exit via a new pathway.
The Na^+ gradient will dissipate, eventually stopping the secondary active absorption of glucose.
GLUT2 will move to the apical membrane to allow glucose entry by facilitated diffusion.
The cell will hyperpolarize, increasing the driving force for glucose entry.

q18.prompt: Which of the following is a characteristic of mixed micelles that distinguishes them from chylomicrons?

options.label: They contain triglycerides at their core.
They are packaged in the Golgi apparatus.
They exit the enterocyte via exocytosis into the lacteals.
They are formed in the intestinal lumen to solubilize hydrophobic lipids.
They require apoprotein B for their structural integrity.

q19.prompt: A patient with Celiac disease has significant atrophy of the villi in the proximal small intestine. Which of the following is the most likely clinical finding?

options.label: Increased absorption of iron and folate due to loss of inhibitory feedback.
Hyperchlorhydria due to a lack of secretin-mediated inhibition of the stomach.
Decreased absorption of all macronutrients due to a reduction in total surface area.
An increase in the bile acid pool size due to faster recirculation.
Rapid gastric emptying caused by the excessive release of CCK.

q20.prompt: Which brush-border enzyme is specifically responsible for debranching starch by breaking alpha-1,6-glycosidic bonds?

options.label: Alpha-amylase.
Alpha-dextrinase.
Maltase.
Sucrase.
Trehalase.
